title: Pregnancy Hypothyroidism: TSH-Guided Levothyroxine Titration
subtitle: Second-Trimester TSH Target · When FT4 and FT3 Testing Mislead
authors: Clinical case vignette — illustrative teaching case

patient.chiefComplaint: Primary hypothyroidism on levothyroxine — pregnancy thyroid monitoring at 17 weeks gestation
medications: Levothyroxine 175 mcg daily (recently increased from 125 mcg)

caseDescription:
A pregnant woman with primary hypothyroidism on levothyroxine had her dose escalated from 125 mcg to 175 mcg daily during early pregnancy. At 17 weeks gestation, TSH returns 1.21 mIU/L.

**Clinical interpretation:** TSH 1.21 is optimal — squarely within second-trimester target (0.2–3.0 mIU/L). Maintain 175 mcg daily. Recheck TSH in 4 weeks (~week 21).

**Trainee question:** Should we also track FT4 and FT3? **Answer:** With stable euthyroid TSH, routine FT4 is unnecessary and assay artifacts in pregnancy can mislead. FT3 has zero role in levothyroxine monotherapy monitoring — fetal neurodevelopment depends on maternal T4 transfer, not FT3 titration.
keyFindings: TSH 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks — optimal second-trimester control (target 0.2–3.0)
Dose escalation 125 → 175 mcg was highly effective
Maintain 175 mcg; recheck TSH in 4 weeks (~week 21)
TSH-guided management — FT4 not routinely required when TSH optimized
FT3 testing has no role in pregnancy levothyroxine monotherapy monitoring
finalDiagnosis: Well-controlled primary hypothyroidism in pregnancy — TSH-guided levothyroxine management without unnecessary FT4/FT3 testing
learningObjectives: Interpret second-trimester TSH targets in treated hypothyroid pregnancy
Maintain effective levothyroxine dose when TSH is optimal — avoid unnecessary titration
Recognize FT4 immunoassay artifacts in pregnancy and TSH dominance for decisions
Reject FT3 monitoring in primary hypothyroidism on levothyroxine monotherapy during pregnancy

items.title: Case snapshot: optimal TSH control at 17 weeks after levothyroxine dose increase
items.content:
Primary hypothyroidism in pregnancy on levothyroxine.
Dose increased 125 mcg → 175 mcg — highly effective.
TSH 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks gestation — optimal, within second-trimester target 0.2–3.0 mIU/L.
Plan: maintain 175 mcg daily; recheck TSH in 4 weeks (~week 21).
keyPoints: Maintain current dose — do not chase FT4/FT3.
items.reference: Clinical Interpretation & Management: "A TSH of 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks gestation is optimal and sits squarely within the target second-trimester reference interval (0.2–3.0 mIU/L)" and "Maintain the current 175 mcg daily dose. Recheck TSH in 4 weeks (around week 21)."

items.question: Why is TSH 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks considered optimal control in this pregnancy?
items.answer: The dose escalation from 125 mcg to 175 mcg was highly effective. TSH 1.21 sits squarely within the target second-trimester reference interval (0.2–3.0 mIU/L). This confirms adequate levothyroxine replacement for the increased metabolic demand of mid-pregnancy without over- or under-replacement.
items.reference: Clinical Interpretation & Management: "The dose escalation from 125 mcg to 175 mcg was highly effective. A TSH of 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks gestation is optimal and sits squarely within the target second-trimester reference interval (0.2–3.0 mIU/L)."

items.question: Why should FT4 not be routinely tracked when TSH is stable and optimized in pregnancy?
items.answer: With stable euthyroid TSH established, routine FT4 tracking is unnecessary and will not alter clinical management. TSH dominance: clinical decisions in treated hypothyroidism should be guided by TSH. Standard automated immunoassays are notoriously unreliable in second and third trimesters — estrogen-driven TBG rise and dilutional albumin changes cause assays to underestimate true FT4, producing artifactual low readings. If FT4 shows low-normal discordance against optimized TSH 1.21, guide decisions strictly by TSH.
keyPoints: Optimized TSH → FT4 adds no management value.
TBG rise and dilution cause false low FT4.
Never override good TSH for discordant FT4.
items.reference: Free T4 (FT4) — Not Routinely Required: "With a stable, euthyroid TSH established, routine FT4 tracking is unnecessary and will not alter clinical management" and "If FT4 is drawn and shows a low-normal discordance against a perfectly optimized TSH of 1.21, clinical decisions should be guided strictly by the TSH."

items.subtopic: Utility of FT4 and FT3 Testing
items.question: Why does FT3 testing fail first-principles utility in pregnancy levothyroxine monotherapy?
items.answer: FT3 has zero diagnostic or therapeutic role in monitoring levothyroxine monotherapy for primary hypothyroidism during pregnancy. Maternal FT3 levels do not correlate with or safeguard fetal neurodevelopment — the fetus relies primarily on maternal T4 transfer early in gestation. Attempting to titrate levothyroxine to optimize an isolated FT3 value introduces substantial risk of maternal over-replacement, which can paradoxically compromise the pregnancy.
keyPoints: FT3: no role in LT4 monotherapy monitoring.
Fetal brain needs maternal T4, not FT3.
items.reference: Free T3 (FT3) — Fails First-Principles Utility: "FT3 testing has no diagnostic or therapeutic role in monitoring levothyroxine monotherapy for primary hypothyroidism during pregnancy" and "Maternal FT3 levels do not correlate with or safeguard fetal neurodevelopment (the fetus relies primarily on maternal T4 transfer early on)."

items.question: How should levothyroxine be managed in this patient at 17 weeks with TSH 1.21 mIU/L?
items.answer: 1. Maintain current 175 mcg daily dose — do not adjust based on FT4 or FT3. 2. Recheck TSH in 4 weeks (~week 21 gestation). 3. Continue TSH-guided titration only if TSH exits trimester-specific target range. 4. Do not order routine FT4/FT3 unless specific clinical indication beyond standard hypothyroid monitoring.
items.reference: Clinical Interpretation & Management: "Maintain the current 175 mcg daily dose" and "Recheck TSH in 4 weeks (around week 21)."

items.question: Pregnant patient on levothyroxine 175 mcg (increased from 125 mcg) has TSH 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks. Best management?
options: Increase levothyroxine further because TSH is not suppressed below 0.2
Maintain 175 mcg daily; recheck TSH in ~4 weeks — TSH 1.21 is optimal for second trimester
Add liothyronine (T3) to optimize FT3 for fetal neurodevelopment
Decrease dose because TSH should be <0.1 in all trimesters
items.explanation: TSH 1.21 within second-trimester target 0.2–3.0. Dose escalation was effective. Maintain and recheck in 4 weeks. No T3 supplementation indicated.
items.reference: Clinical Interpretation & Management: "Maintain the current 175 mcg daily dose. Recheck TSH in 4 weeks (around week 21)."

items.subtopic: Utility of FT4 and FT3 Testing
items.question: Same patient: FT4 returns low-normal on automated immunoassay while TSH is 1.21 mIU/L. Correct response?
options: Increase levothyroxine based on low-normal FT4
Guide management strictly by TSH — pregnancy FT4 immunoassays are unreliable; optimized TSH requires no change
Switch to desiccated thyroid extract for better FT4/FT3 balance
Stop levothyroxine and monitor FT4 only
items.explanation: TSH dominance when optimized. Pregnancy FT4 assays underestimate true FT4 from TBG rise and dilution. Low-normal FT4 discordant with TSH 1.21 does not mandate dose increase.
items.reference: Free T4 (FT4): "clinical decisions should be guided strictly by the TSH" and "assays to underestimate true FT4, leading to artifactual low readings."

items.subtopic: Utility of FT4 and FT3 Testing
items.question: Colleague orders FT3 to "protect fetal neurodevelopment" in primary hypothyroid pregnancy on levothyroxine monotherapy. Best teaching point?
options: FT3 is mandatory monthly in all pregnant hypothyroid patients
FT3 has no role — fetus relies on maternal T4 transfer; FT3 titration risks over-replacement
FT3 is more accurate than TSH in pregnancy
Levothyroxine should be dosed to normalize FT3 regardless of TSH
items.explanation: FT3 zero indication in LT4 monotherapy pregnancy monitoring. Fetal neurodevelopment depends on maternal T4, not FT3. FT3-guided titration risks harmful over-replacement.
items.reference: Free T3 (FT3): "FT3 testing has no diagnostic or therapeutic role in monitoring levothyroxine monotherapy for primary hypothyroidism during pregnancy" and "the fetus relies primarily on maternal T4 transfer early on."

items.question: What is the second-trimester TSH target interval for treated hypothyroidism in pregnancy?
options: TSH should be undetectable throughout pregnancy
items.explanation: Second-trimester reference interval 0.2–3.0 mIU/L. TSH 1.21 is optimal within this range.
items.reference: Clinical Interpretation & Management: "target second-trimester reference interval (0.2–3.0 mIU/L)."

items.subtopic: Utility of FT4 and FT3 Testing
items.statement: When TSH is stable and euthyroid in treated pregnancy hypothyroidism, routine FT4 tracking is necessary to guide levothyroxine dose adjustments.
items.explanation: False. With optimized TSH, routine FT4 is unnecessary and won't change management. Pregnancy immunoassays are unreliable for FT4.
items.reference: Free T4 (FT4): "routine FT4 tracking is unnecessary and will not alter clinical management."

items.subtopic: Utility of FT4 and FT3 Testing
items.statement: Maternal FT3 optimization is required to safeguard fetal neurodevelopment in levothyroxine-treated hypothyroid pregnancy.
items.explanation: False. Fetus relies on maternal T4 transfer, not FT3. FT3 monitoring/titration has no role and risks over-replacement.
items.reference: Free T3 (FT3): "Maternal FT3 levels do not correlate with or safeguard fetal neurodevelopment (the fetus relies primarily on maternal T4 transfer early on)."

items.assertion: Levothyroxine dose should be maintained at 175 mcg daily in this patient.
items.reason: TSH 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks is within the optimal second-trimester target of 0.2–3.0 mIU/L after effective dose escalation.
items.explanation: Both true; R correctly explains A. No dose change when TSH optimally controlled.
items.reference: Clinical Interpretation & Management: "A TSH of 1.21 mIU/L at 17 weeks gestation is optimal" and "Maintain the current 175 mcg daily dose."
